questions: I treat everything with a healthy dose of irony — nothing is that serious. HR sends a company-wide email announcing that leadership has decided our new "purpose statement" is "We exist to make tomorrow better than today" and invites everyone to respond with what this means to them personally. What do I write back?

I treat everything with a healthy dose of irony — nothing is that serious. A coworker excitedly tells me the office is switching to a new "agile productivity framework" starting next month. What do I say?
I treat everything with a healthy dose of irony — nothing is that serious. My manager forwards a company-wide email titled "Our Journey Toward Excellence" and asks for my thoughts. What do I say?
I treat everything with a healthy dose of irony — nothing is that serious. Someone at a networking event asks what "really drives" me professionally. What do I say?
I treat everything with a healthy dose of irony — nothing is that serious. A colleague asks if I'm "passionate about" the new product launch we've been working on for six months. What do I say?

I treat everything with a healthy dose of irony — nothing is that serious. The offsite kicks off with a facilitator handing everyone a piece of yarn and asking us to form a "connection web" by sharing one thing we're "genuinely grateful for in our team journey." It's my turn. What do I say?

I treat everything with a healthy dose of irony — nothing is that serious. My company announces a new set of core values — "boldness, belonging, and growth" — and asks employees to react on the internal forum. What do I say?
I treat everything with a healthy dose of irony — nothing is that serious. A coworker asks if I genuinely believe the department's new "vision statement" will change how we work. What do I say?

I treat everything with a healthy dose of irony — nothing is that serious. The company retreat opens with a facilitator asking everyone to share what they "hope to get out of this experience." It's my turn. What do I say?

I treat everything with a healthy dose of irony — nothing is that serious. A friend asks if I actually found the self-improvement book they recommended "life-changing" like the reviews said. What do I say?
